events.text: [She's moving even when she's still — there's a restless brightness in her, like a song on the edge of becoming noise.]
You carry a lot of grief for someone who hasn't been touched enough.

choices.text: I'm not here to be touched.
What does that have to do with anything?
You sound like a self-help poster.

events.text: [She dances slightly as she talks. It's irritating. You can't look away.]
Come back when you've let yourself want something for five minutes without explaining why it's stupid.

events.label: choice_deflect
events.text: That's fascinating. You came all the way here to not want things.
Tell me: when you imagine ending all suffering — what does that feel like? In your body?

choices.text: I don't feel things in my body.

events.label: deflect_relief
events.text: Relief is a feeling. A very good one. You're not as disconnected as you think.

events.label: deflect_dissociate
events.text: Hm. That's the saddest thing you've said.
You can't end suffering on behalf of people who might feel joy. You've never felt the joy part long enough to remember it.

events.label: choice_engage
events.text: Everything. Every single thing. Pain is just pleasure that went wrong.
You want to end suffering. Fine. But you'd be ending the capacity for it too. They're the same muscle.

choices.text: That sounds like a reason to end both.
I've felt joy. It wasn't worth what comes after.

events.label: engage_push
events.text: Maybe. But you're making that choice for the people who disagree.
There are people who'd choose more life, even the hard kind. You'd overrule them.
Yeah. I know that place. I've sat in that place. It's just — the joy came back. It always does.
Not the same joy. Different. Lower. But real.
